title: Machine Learning Portfolio
text:
Welcome
This is a portfolio of statistical modeling and machine-learning projects built in R and Python. Each chapter takes a real dataset, builds and evaluates models, and rebuilds every figure as an interactive plotly visualization — paired with a plain-language explanation of what the analysis is doing and why.

title: Machine Learning Portfolio
text:
What’s inside
The work is grouped into two parts: foundational statistical modeling in R, and machine-learning models built in Python.

Part I — Data Analysis & Regression (R)
Built with R, tidyverse, glmnet, and FNN.

title: Machine Learning Portfolio
text:
How to read this portfolio

Use the sidebar to jump between chapters, or the search box (top of the sidebar) to find a topic.
Most charts are interactive — hover for tooltips, click legend entries to toggle series, and drag to zoom.
Code is folded by default. Click ▸ Code above any output to reveal the source, or use the code menu (top-right) to show all code at once.

title: Machine Learning Portfolio
text:
Reproducibility
Every chapter is self-contained and fully reproducible — each one loads its own data and computes its results at render time. Datasets live in the Files/ directory of the source repository; the machine-learning chapters pull benchmark datasets directly from the UCI Machine Learning Repository.

title: 1  Data Wrangling: Baseball Career Records
text:
1.1 Baseball Records
This project uses dplyr to query the historical Lahman Baseball Database, surfacing the small groups of players who reached rare career milestones — combining different elite skills, dominating across decades, or sustaining production over very long careers. It demonstrates data-wrangling fundamentals: grouping, aggregating, joining, and filtering across millions of records.
Aggregating career totals across the full Lahman database, these tables identify the players who cleared legendary statistical thresholds.
crumbs: 1  Data Wrangling: Baseball Career Records

title: 2  Linear Regression & Model Selection
text:
2.1 Q1 — Insurance Charges
This project works through model selection and interpretation across three datasets: comparing nested linear models on insurance charges, applying a prediction equation to used-car prices, and performing EDA plus regression on Northern Virginia housing.
Five nested models are fit, from age alone up to all six predictors, then compared on both training fit and held-out validation error.

title: 2  Linear Regression & Model Selection
text:
Training R²Validation MetricsValidation Table




Code
r2_df <- data.frame(
  Model  = factor(ins_labels, levels = ins_labels),
  R2     = ins_val_metrics$Train_R2,
  R2_adj = ins_val_metrics$Train_R2_adj
)
r2_long <- tidyr::pivot_longer(r2_df, cols = c(R2, R2_adj), names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value")

p_r2 <- ggplot(r2_long, aes(x = Model, y = Value, fill = Metric)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("R2" = "#4e79a7", "R2_adj" = "#f28e2b"),
                    labels = c("R2" = "R²", "R2_adj" = "Adjusted R²")) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = function(x) gsub("\\+", "+\n", x)) +
  labs(title = "Training R² vs Adjusted R² by Model",
       x = "Model Predictors", y = "R²", fill = "Metric") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8))

ggplotly(p_r2, tooltip = c("x", "y", "fill"))






Each pair of bars shows R² (blue) and Adjusted R² (orange) on the training set for each of the five nested models, from the simplest (age only) to the most complex (all six predictors). R² always increases as predictors are added — even irrelevant ones — while Adjusted R² penalizes for model complexity. The gap between them grows as the models become more complex, signaling diminishing returns. Adding smoker in Model 3 produces the largest single jump, indicating it is by far the strongest predictor of insurance charges.




Code
val_long <- tidyr::pivot_longer(
  ins_val_metrics[, c("Model", "Validation_RMSE", "Validation_MAE")],
  cols = c(Validation_RMSE, Validation_MAE),
  names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value"
)
val_long$Model <- factor(val_long$Model, levels = ins_labels)

p_val <- ggplot(val_long, aes(x = Model, y = Value, color = Metric, group = Metric)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = function(x) paste0("M", seq_along(ins_labels))[match(x, ins_labels)]) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Validation_RMSE" = "#e15759", "Validation_MAE" = "#4e79a7"),
                     labels = c("Validation_RMSE" = "RMSE", "Validation_MAE" = "MAE")) +
  labs(title = "Validation RMSE & MAE Across 5 Models",
       x = "Model (M1 = simplest → M5 = full)", y = "Error ($)", color = "Metric") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

ggplotly(p_val, tooltip = c("x", "y", "colour"))






Both RMSE and MAE are computed on the held-out 20% validation set — lower means better out-of-sample predictions. M1 (age only) has the highest error. Both metrics drop sharply at M3 when smoker enters, then level off, confirming that smoking status dominates predictive power. The relatively small further drop from M3 to M5 suggests the extra predictors (sex, region) add limited value beyond what age, BMI, smoking, and children already capture.




Code
knitr::kable(
  ins_val_metrics,
  col.names = c("Model Predictors", "Train R²", "Train Adj. R²",
                "Val. MSE", "Val. RMSE", "Val. MAE"),
  caption = "Training fit statistics and validation error for all five insurance charge models."
)

title: 2  Linear Regression & Model Selection
text:
2.2 Q2 — Used Car Price Prediction
A prediction equation derived from a training set is applied to a validation set of used-car listings.

Predicted vs Actual




Code
p_cars <- ggplot(val_cars, aes(x = Price, y = Predicted_Price, color = Model,
                                text = paste0("Model: ", Model,
                                              "<br>Miles: ", Miles,
                                              "<br>Actual: $", Price, "K",
                                              "<br>Predicted: $", round(Predicted_Price, 2), "K"))) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Prius" = "#4e79a7", "Tacoma" = "#f28e2b", "Volt" = "#59a14f")) +
  labs(title = "Used Car: Predicted vs Actual Price",
       x = "Actual Price ($K)", y = "Predicted Price ($K)", color = "Car Model") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

ggplotly(p_cars, tooltip = "text")






Each point is one used-car listing from the validation set: the model’s predicted price (y-axis) versus the car’s actual listed price (x-axis), colored by car model (Prius, Tacoma, or Volt). The red dashed line marks perfect prediction. Points close to the line were predicted accurately; points above or below represent over- or under-predictions. The three car models occupy distinct price regions, reflecting the dummy-variable structure of the regression.









Key Findings — Used Cars



Regression Equation: Price = 18.69496 − 0.07807(Miles) + 9.45872(Tacoma) − 11.02341(Volt)
Validation RMSE: 3.2695 ($K)

Every additional mile driven reduces predicted price by ~$78.
A Tacoma is predicted to sell for ~$9,459 more than the baseline (Prius), holding miles equal.
A Volt is predicted to sell for ~$11,023 less than the Prius, holding miles equal.

title: 2  Linear Regression & Model Selection
text:
2.3 Q3 & Q4 — NOVA Housing EDA
Exploratory analysis of Northern Virginia housing: identifying outliers, log-transforming price, and a first regression on bathroom count.

Price DistributionlogPrice DistributionlogPrice vs BathroomsLargest-Bathroom Residual




Code
p_price <- ggplot(nova4, aes(x = Price)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 200000, color = "black", fill = "lightblue") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 3000000, color = "red", linewidth = 1.2, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(data = nova4_outliers, aes(x = Price, y = 0),
             color = "red", shape = 1, size = 8) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Property Prices",
       subtitle = "Red dashed line = $3M cutoff; circled points are outliers",
       x = "Price (dollars)", y = "Number of Properties") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

ggplotly(p_price)






This histogram shows the full distribution of asking prices across all Northern Virginia listings. The x-axis is in dollars; the y-axis counts properties in each bin. The red dashed vertical line marks the $3,000,000 cutoff, and the circled red points at the base highlight the handful of properties above it. These ultra-luxury outliers are concentrated in Arlington and Fairfax counties and have unusually large living square footage or lot sizes. Because they would disproportionately influence model fitting, they are removed before analysis.




Code
p_logprice <- ggplot(nova4_clean, aes(x = logPrice)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.1, color = "black", fill = "#4e79a7", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of log(Price) — Cleaned Dataset",
       x = "log(Price)", y = "Number of Properties") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

ggplotly(p_logprice)






After removing the $3M+ outliers and properties from Loudoun and Prince William counties, log-transforming price produces this much more symmetric, bell-shaped distribution. A symmetric distribution is important for linear regression — it satisfies the normality assumption for residuals and reduces the influence of extreme values. The transformation from raw dollars to log-dollars brings the skewed right tail of housing prices into a well-behaved range.




Code
p_bath <- ggplot(nova4_clean, aes(x = Bathrooms, y = logPrice)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#4e79a7", size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "red", linetype = "solid") +
  labs(title = "log(Price) vs Number of Bathrooms",
       x = "Bathrooms", y = "log(Price)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

ggplotly(p_bath)






Each point is one property: log(Price) on the y-axis versus bathroom count on the x-axis. The red line is the fitted simple linear regression with a 95% confidence band. A clear upward trend is visible — more bathrooms are associated with higher prices — and the relationship is reasonably linear on the log-price scale. Pearson correlation: r = 0.6905. The regression line is: logPrice = 12.567869 + 0.249756(Bathrooms), explaining 47.69% of variation in log(Price).




Code
p_resid <- ggplot(nova4_clean, aes(x = Bathrooms, y = logPrice)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "#4e79a7", size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "blue") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = max_bath_obs$Bathrooms, xend = max_bath_obs$Bathrooms,
                   y = max_bath_pred, yend = max_bath_obs$logPrice),
               color = "red", linewidth = 1.5) +
  geom_point(data = max_bath_obs, aes(x = Bathrooms, y = logPrice),
             color = "red", size = 4, shape = 17) +
  labs(title = "Residual Highlighted for Maximum Bathrooms",
       x = "Bathrooms", y = "log(Price)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

ggplotly(p_resid)






The blue line is the fitted regression. The red triangle marks the property with the most bathrooms in the dataset, and the red vertical segment is its residual — the gap between what the model predicted (bottom of segment) and its actual log-price (top). A large residual here means the property’s price is not fully explained by bathroom count alone, motivating the addition of more predictors like the Arlington indicator in Question 5.

title: 2  Linear Regression & Model Selection
text:
2.4 Q5 — Multiple Regression
Adding an Arlington county indicator to the bathroom model to control for location.

Regression FitModel Summary




Code
p_multi <- ggplot(nova4_clean, aes(x = Bathrooms, y = logPrice, color = factor(Arlington))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, size = 1.3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("0" = "#4e79a7", "1" = "#e15759"),
                     labels = c("0" = "Fairfax", "1" = "Arlington")) +
  labs(title = "log(Price) ~ Bathrooms + Arlington",
       x = "Bathrooms", y = "log(Price)", color = "County") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

ggplotly(p_multi, tooltip = c("x", "y", "colour"))






Points are colored by county (Arlington in red, Fairfax in blue); the two parallel lines are the fitted regression for each group. Because the model includes only main effects and no interaction, the lines are forced to have the same slope — the Arlington coefficient shifts the Fairfax line upward by a fixed amount. The vertical gap between the two lines is the estimated Arlington premium: Arlington homes are predicted to sell for 61.4% more than comparable Fairfax homes.




Code
coef_df <- data.frame(
  Term     = c("Intercept", "Bathrooms", "Arlington"),
  Estimate = c(multi_int, multi_bath_b, multi_arl_b),
  Interpretation = c(
    "Predicted log(Price) when Bathrooms=0 and Fairfax",
    paste0("logPrice increases by ", multi_bath_b, " per additional bathroom (Arlington held constant)"),
    paste0("Arlington homes have logPrice ", multi_arl_b, " higher than Fairfax (Bathrooms held constant)")
  )
)
knitr::kable(coef_df, digits = 6,
             caption = "logPrice ~ Bathrooms + Arlington — coefficient estimates and interpretations")

title: 3  Regularization & k-Nearest Neighbors
text:
3.1 Q1 — MLB Salary Prediction
This project focuses on model selection and flexibility: using regularization (LASSO) and stepwise selection to predict baseball salaries, contrasting a rigid linear fit against a flexible k-nearest-neighbors smoother on synthetic data, and tuning kNN on real housing data.
Three approaches to predicting player salary are compared: LASSO at two penalty strengths and stepwise backward elimination.

title: 3  Regularization & k-Nearest Neighbors
text:
Model ComparisonLASSO Cross-ValidationPredicted vs Actual




Code
model_df <- data.frame(
  Model      = c("LASSO (λ.min)", "LASSO (λ.1se)", "Backward Elimination"),
  RMSE       = c(rmse_min, rmse_1se, rmse_back),
  Predictors = c(coef_min_nz, coef_1se_nz, coef_back_nz)
)

p <- ggplot(model_df, aes(x = reorder(Model, RMSE), y = RMSE, fill = Model)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.55, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(RMSE, 1)), vjust = -0.4, size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#4e79a7", "#f28e2b", "#59a14f")) +
  labs(title = "Validation RMSE by Model", x = "Model", y = "RMSE (thousands $)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

ggplotly(p, tooltip = c("x", "y"))






Each bar shows the root-mean-square error (in $1,000s) of a model’s salary predictions on the held-out 15% validation set — lower is better. Three approaches are compared: the two LASSO models (penalty tuned by cross-validation at λ.min and λ.1se) and stepwise backward elimination. Backward elimination has the shortest bar, meaning its predictions were, on average, closest to players’ actual salaries on unseen data.




Code
plot(cv_lasso, main = "Cross-Validated MSE vs log(λ)")









This plot is the diagnostic LASSO uses to select its penalty strength λ. The x-axis is log(λ) and the y-axis is cross-validated prediction error; red dots show the average error at each λ with error bars. The two dashed vertical lines mark λ.min (the penalty that minimizes error) and λ.1se (the simplest model within one standard error of the best). The numbers across the top count how many predictors remain active — as λ grows, LASSO progressively shrinks more coefficients to exactly zero, producing sparser models.




Code
pva_df <- data.frame(Actual = y_valid_mlb, Predicted = as.numeric(pred_back))

p2 <- ggplot(pva_df, aes(x = Actual, y = Predicted)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, color = "#4e79a7") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Backward Elimination: Predicted vs Actual",
       x = "Actual Salary (thousands $)", y = "Predicted Salary (thousands $)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

ggplotly(p2)






For the best-performing backward-elimination model, each point is one player from the validation set: predicted salary (y-axis) versus their actual salary (x-axis). The red dashed line marks perfect prediction — points on it were predicted exactly. The cluster near the line at lower salary levels shows good accuracy for most players, while the wider spread at high salaries reflects greater uncertainty for the highest-paid stars.

title: 3  Regularization & k-Nearest Neighbors
text:
3.2 Q2 — Linear Regression vs kNN
A deliberately non-linear synthetic dataset exposes the difference between a parametric and a non-parametric model.

Scatterplot + Fitted CurvesLinear Model Summary




Code
pred_df <- data.frame(x = x_grid, LinReg = lm_preds, kNN = knn_preds)

p3 <- ggplot(df_sim, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "black", size = 1.5) +
  geom_line(data = pred_df, aes(x = x, y = LinReg, color = "Linear Regression"), linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_line(data = pred_df, aes(x = x, y = kNN,    color = "kNN (k=10)"),         linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Linear Regression" = "red", "kNN (k=10)" = "blue")) +
  labs(title = "Scatterplot with Fitted Curves", x = "x", y = "y", color = "Model") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

ggplotly(p3)






The black dots are 250 raw data points generated with deliberately complex, non-linear structure (sinusoidal for x < 0, quadratic for x ∈ [0, 7], noisy for x ∈ [7, 9], and linear-with-jumps beyond x = 9). The red line is ordinary linear regression — forced into a single straight line, it cannot track the underlying curves. The blue line is a from-scratch kNN smoother (k = 10), which predicts each point by averaging its 10 nearest neighbors and visibly hugs the local shape of the data. The contrast makes the flexibility advantage of a non-parametric method visually clear.




Code
summary_df <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("Intercept", "Slope", "R²"),
  Value  = c(lm_intercept, lm_slope, lm_r2)
)
knitr::kable(summary_df, digits = 4, caption = "SLR: y ~ x")



SLR: y ~ x



Metric
Value




(Intercept)
Intercept
3.0483


x
Slope
-0.5190



R²
0.0608





This table reports the fitted simple linear regression of y on x. The intercept is the predicted y-value when x = 0. The slope is the average change in y for every one-unit increase in x. R² measures the share of variation in y explained by the straight-line fit — a low value here is expected and informative, quantifying just how poorly a single line captures this strongly nonlinear data.









Key Findings — Linear Regression vs kNN



SLR Model: y = 3.0483 + -0.519x  |  R² = 0.0608

Where does linear regression fail? The model performs most poorly in the strongly nonlinear regions — for negative x values and around the sharp transitions near x = 0 and x = 9.
Parametric vs non-parametric: Linear regression is parametric — it assumes a fixed straight-line form regardless of the data’s true shape. kNN is non-parametric — the shape emerges entirely from the data. The trade-off: linear regression is simpler and more interpretable; kNN is more flexible but harder to communicate.
Effect of decreasing k to 1: The kNN curve would become much more jagged, closely tracking individual points rather than the underlying trend. This increases flexibility but raises the risk of overfitting — memorizing noise instead of signal.

title: 4  Gaussian Naïve Bayes
text:
4.1 Feature Distributions
This chapter implements Gaussian Naïve Bayes from scratch on the classic Iris dataset — computing class-wise Gaussian likelihoods by hand rather than calling a library classifier — and visualizes every step from the learned distributions through to the final confusion matrix.
The model learns one Gaussian curve per (species, feature). These visualizations show what it learned.

title: 4  Gaussian Naïve Bayes
text:
Gaussian PDFs by ClassClass Statistics Table




Code
colors = {'Iris-setosa': '#1f77b4', 'Iris-versicolor': '#ff7f0e', 'Iris-virginica': '#2ca02c'}
short  = {'Iris-setosa': 'Setosa', 'Iris-versicolor': 'Versicolor', 'Iris-virginica': 'Virginica'}

fig = go.Figure()
shown = set()
for feat_idx, feat in enumerate(features):
    visible = (feat_idx == 0)
    for c in classes:
        mu, sig = mean_dict[c][feat], sd_dict[c][feat]
        xs = np.linspace(mu - 4 * sig, mu + 4 * sig, 300)
        ys = normal_dist(xs, mu, sig)
        label = short[c]
        show_legend = (label not in shown) and visible
        if visible:
            shown.add(label)
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=xs, y=ys, mode='lines', name=label,
            line=dict(color=colors[c], width=2.5),
            visible=visible, legendgroup=label, showlegend=show_legend
        ))


                        
                                            


Code
n = len(classes)
buttons = []
for fi, feat in enumerate(features):
    vis = [False] * (len(features) * n)
    for j in range(n):
        vis[fi * n + j] = True
    buttons.append(dict(label=feat.title(), method='update',
                        args=[{'visible': vis},

                               'xaxis': {'title': feat.title()}}]))

fig.update_layout(
    title='Gaussian PDFs — Sepal Length',
    xaxis_title='Sepal Length (cm)', yaxis_title='Probability Density',
    updatemenus=[dict(buttons=buttons, direction='down',
                      x=0.01, xanchor='left', y=1.15, yanchor='top', showactive=True)],
    legend_title='Species', template='plotly_white', height=420
)


                        
                                            


Code
fig.show()


                        
                                            


Each curve is the Gaussian (normal) probability density function learned from the training data for one species and one feature. The mean (center) and standard deviation (width) are estimated directly from the training samples for that class. The dropdown lets you switch between all four Iris features. Where the curves for different species overlap, the classifier is less certain; where they are well-separated (notably petal length and petal width), the classifier can discriminate with high confidence. This is the core of Gaussian Naïve Bayes — it assumes each feature is normally distributed within each class, and uses these learned curves to compute likelihoods at prediction time.




Code
rows = []
for c in classes:
    for f in features:
        rows.append({
            'Species': c.replace('Iris-', '').capitalize(),
            'Feature': f.title(),
            'Mean': round(mean_dict[c][f], 4),
            'Std Dev': round(sd_dict[c][f], 4),
            'Prior P(class)': round(prior_dict[c], 4)
        })
stats_df = pd.DataFrame(rows)

fig2 = go.Figure(data=[go.Table(
    header=dict(values=list(stats_df.columns), fill_color='#4e79a7',
                font=dict(color='white', size=13), align='left'),
    cells=dict(values=[stats_df[c] for c in stats_df.columns],
               fill_color=[['#f0f4f8' if i % 2 == 0 else 'white' for i in range(len(stats_df))]],
               align='left', font=dict(size=12))
)])
fig2.update_layout(title='Learned Parameters — GNB Model', height=480, template='plotly_white')


                        
                                            


Code
fig2.show()


                        
                                            


This table lists every parameter the Gaussian Naïve Bayes model learns during training: the mean and standard deviation for each (species, feature) combination, plus the prior probability P(class) — the fraction of training samples belonging to each species. At prediction time, these numbers fully determine the model: for a new flower, GNB evaluates the Gaussian PDF at the observed feature value using the stored mean and SD for each class, multiplies the four feature likelihoods together (the “naïve” independence assumption), scales by the prior, and predicts whichever class yields the highest result.

4.2 Manual Calculation
Walking through a single prediction by hand for one test flower (sepal = 7.3, 2.9; petal = 6.3, 1.8).

                fill_color='#4e79a7', font=dict(color='white', size=13), align='left'),
    cells=dict(values=[manual_df['Feature'], manual_df['Value'],
                       manual_df['setosa'], manual_df['versicolor'], manual_df['virginica']],

                   height=280, template='plotly_white')

fig3.show()


                        
                                            


This table walks through the first step of a Gaussian Naïve Bayes prediction by hand for a single test flower with sepal length = 7.3, sepal width = 2.9, petal length = 6.3, petal width = 1.8 (a known Iris-virginica). For each of the four features, the Gaussian PDF is evaluated at the observed value using the class-specific mean and standard deviation learned from the training data. The resulting number, P(x | class), is the likelihood of seeing that feature value if the flower truly belonged to that class. Very small values (like those for Setosa on petal length) are strong evidence against that class.




Code
joint_df = pd.DataFrame({'Species': list(joint.keys()), 'Joint Score': list(joint.values())})

fig4 = px.bar(
    joint_df, x='Species', y='Joint Score', color='Species',
    color_discrete_map={'setosa': '#1f77b4', 'versicolor': '#ff7f0e', 'virginica': '#2ca02c'},
    text=joint_df['Joint Score'].apply(lambda v: f'{v:.2e}'),
    title='Unnormalized Joint Score per Class — Example Point',
    labels={'Joint Score': 'P(class) × ∏ P(xᵢ | class)'}
)
fig4.update_traces(textposition='outside')


                        
                                            


Code
fig4.update_layout(showlegend=False, template='plotly_white', height=400,
                   yaxis_title='Joint Probability (P(class) · ∏ P(xᵢ|class))')


                        
                                            


Code
fig4.show()


                        
                                            


After computing the per-feature likelihoods, GNB multiplies them all together with the class prior: P(class) × P(x₁|class) × P(x₂|class) × P(x₃|class) × P(x₄|class). The result is an unnormalized score proportional to the posterior probability of each class. The bar chart makes the decision visually obvious — Virginica scores orders of magnitude higher than the other two classes, so the classifier confidently predicts Iris-virginica. This matches the true label for this observation.

title: 4  Gaussian Naïve Bayes
text:
4.4 Key Findings






How Gaussian Naïve Bayes Works



Gaussian Naïve Bayes is a probabilistic classifier that combines two ideas:

Bayes’ theorem: P(class | features) ∝ P(features | class) × P(class)
Naïve independence assumption: treat each feature as conditionally independent given the class, so the joint likelihood factors into a product:

\[P(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4 \mid class) = \prod_{i=1}^{4} P(x_i \mid class)\]
Each conditional likelihood P(xᵢ | class) is modeled as a Gaussian distribution whose mean μ and standard deviation σ are estimated directly from the training data for each class.

Training: compute μ and σ for every (class, feature) pair → store as a lookup table.
Prediction: for a new flower, evaluate the Gaussian PDF at each observed feature value, multiply across features, scale by the class prior, and pick the class with the highest score. Log-probabilities are used in practice to avoid numerical underflow from multiplying many small numbers.

title: 6  Random Forests & Feature Importance
text:
6.1 Gini Impurity & Data
This project explores the mechanics behind decision trees and random forests: how a split is chosen by minimizing Gini impurity, and how two different methods — Mean Decrease in Impurity (MDI) and permutation importance — can disagree about which features matter. (A collaborative project with Sabeeh, Ronish, and Nihal.)

title: 6  Random Forests & Feature Importance
text:
Gini ComparisonBlob Scatter




Code
fig_rf1 = go.Figure(data=[go.Table(
    header=dict(values=list(rf_gini_compare.columns), fill_color="#4e79a7",
                font=dict(color="white", size=13), align="center"),
    cells=dict(values=[rf_gini_compare[c] for c in rf_gini_compare.columns],
               fill_color=[["#f0f4f8" if i % 2 == 0 else "white" for i in range(len(rf_gini_compare))]],
               align="center", font=dict(size=13))
)])
fig_rf1.update_layout(title="Gini Impurity Comparison: Manual Median Split vs Tree-Selected Split",
                      height=230, template="plotly_white")


                        
                                            


Code
fig_rf1.show()


                        
                                            


This table compares two ways of splitting the synthetic 2-class blob dataset. Manual split: the dataset is divided at the median value of feature f1 — a naïve threshold that produces equal class mixtures in both halves (Gini ≈ 0.5 each), meaning no purity improvement. Tree split: the DecisionTreeClassifier automatically searched all features and thresholds to find the split that minimized weighted Gini impurity, landing on feature f3 with a threshold of 3.477. This split achieves left Gini ≈ 0.039 and right Gini ≈ 0.015, giving a weighted Gini of ~0.027 and a purity improvement of ~0.473 — dramatically better. This demonstrates why decision trees perform greedy search over all possible splits rather than using arbitrary thresholds.




Code
fig_rf2 = px.scatter(

The blobs overlap significantly in f1 and f2, which is why the manual median split on f1 found no improvement — both halves still contain a near 50/50 class mix. Feature f3 (not visible in this 2D view) provides much better separation, which is why the decision tree chose it as the splitting feature. title: 6  Random Forests & Feature Importance
text:
6.2 Feature Importance
Two complementary ways to rank feature importance in a trained random forest.

MDIPermutationMDI vs Permutation




Code
rf_mdi_df = pd.DataFrame({"Feature": rf_mdi.index, "MDI Importance": rf_mdi.values})
fig_rf3 = px.bar(
    rf_mdi_df.sort_values("MDI Importance"),
    x="MDI Importance", y="Feature", orientation="h",
    color="MDI Importance", color_continuous_scale="Blues",
    title="Random Forest — MDI Feature Importance (100 Trees)",
    text=rf_mdi_df.sort_values("MDI Importance")["MDI Importance"].apply(lambda v: f"{v:.4f}")
)
fig_rf3.update_traces(textposition="outside")


                        
                                            


Code
fig_rf3.update_layout(showlegend=False, template="plotly_white", height=380, coloraxis_showscale=False)


                        
                                            


Code
fig_rf3.show()


                        
                                            


Mean Decrease in Impurity (MDI) measures how much each feature reduces Gini impurity when it is used as a split node, averaged across all 100 trees in the forest. Features used more frequently in high-quality splits receive higher scores. The bars show each feature’s share of total impurity reduction. Note that MDI can be biased toward features with more unique values or features that happen to be correlated with the target — it reflects how much the tree used a feature, not necessarily how important it is for prediction on new data.




Code
rf_perm_df = pd.DataFrame({"Feature": rf_perm.index, "Permutation Importance": rf_perm.values})
fig_rf4 = px.bar(
    rf_perm_df.sort_values("Permutation Importance"),
    x="Permutation Importance", y="Feature", orientation="h",
    color="Permutation Importance", color_continuous_scale="Oranges",
    title="Random Forest — Permutation Importance (10 Repeats, Test Set)",
    text=rf_perm_df.sort_values("Permutation Importance")["Permutation Importance"].apply(lambda v: f"{v:.4f}")
)
fig_rf4.update_traces(textposition="outside")


                        
                                            


Code
fig_rf4.update_layout(showlegend=False, template="plotly_white", height=380, coloraxis_showscale=False)


                        
                                            


Code
fig_rf4.show()


                        
                                            


Permutation importance measures the drop in model accuracy when each feature’s values are randomly shuffled — breaking the relationship between that feature and the target. A large drop means the model depended heavily on that feature for correct predictions; a drop near zero means the feature was not actually needed for good performance. Unlike MDI, permutation importance evaluates the feature’s effect on held-out test data, making it a more reliable indicator of true predictive usefulness and less susceptible to overfitting artifacts.

Code
fig_rf5.show()


                        
                                            


Placing both importance measures side-by-side reveals whether they agree. When MDI is high but permutation importance is low for the same feature, it signals that the tree frequently split on that feature during training (inflating its MDI score) but shuffling it on the test set barely hurt performance — meaning the feature may be redundant or its splits were not truly discriminative. Conversely, features where both measures agree strongly are the most reliable signal of genuine predictive importance.









Key Findings — Random Forest



Dataset: Synthetic 2-class blob data (n=1,000, 6 features, random_state=4)



Split
Weighted Gini
Purity Gain




Manual (median of f1)
0.500
0.000


Tree-chosen (f3 ≤ 3.477)
0.027
0.473




The greedy best-split search in decision trees dramatically outperforms arbitrary threshold choices — a 0.473 purity gain vs 0.000 for the median split.
MDI vs Permutation — these two importance measures can tell different stories. MDI reflects how the model was built; permutation importance reflects what the model actually needs. Features with high MDI but low permutation importance may be redundant or spuriously correlated.
In practice, permutation importance is considered more reliable for assessing true feature predictive power, particularly when features are correlated.
Random forests reduce variance over single decision trees by averaging across many trees trained on bootstrapped data subsets, each considering only a random feature subset per split.

7.1 Cross-Validation Experiments
This chapter uses ROC curves and stratified k-fold cross-validation to predict corporate bankruptcy from financial ratios, exploring how the number of features, a single problematic feature, and different normalization strategies affect classifier performance.

Adding the 11th feature — Operating Expense Rate — causes AUC to drop. The red curve falls below the blue 10-feature curve, meaning the model makes worse predictions when this feature is included. Code
fig_roc3.show()


                        
                                            


Applying normalization before training restores and even improves performance on the 11-feature set. L1 normalization scales each sample so that the sum of absolute feature values equals 1; L2 normalization scales so that the sum of squared feature values equals 1 (unit vector). Both are applied per-sample (axis=1) and followed by StandardScaler to center the features. L2 normalization performed best — it rescales each sample’s magnitude without distorting the direction of the feature vector, preserving the relative relationships between features that the classifier depends on. Normalizing correctly mitigates the harmful effect of the noisy Feature 11.

Key Findings — Bankruptcy Prediction



Dataset: UCI Taiwanese Bankruptcy Prediction (id=572) — 6,819 companies, 95 financial features Method: 10-Fold Stratified Cross-Validation with Logistic Regression Goal: Classify companies as bankrupt (1) or not (0) from financial ratios

Why folds differ with 1 feature: with only one financial ratio, the model is highly sensitive to which specific companies land in each fold — small random variation in the subset composition strongly affects performance.
Why Feature 11 hurts: the Operating Expense Rate column introduced noise or scale inconsistencies that confused the classifier. Normalization neutralizes this by rescaling each sample to a common magnitude before training.
Best normalization — L2: L2 row-normalization rescales each company’s feature vector to unit length, preserving the directional relationships between features while removing magnitude differences. This is the most effective preprocessing step for this linear classifier on this data.


Note: The original lab used a linear SVM; this chapter uses logistic regression for faster rendering, but the qualitative findings — more features help, Feature 11 hurts, L2 normalization recovers performance — are identical.

title: 8  Decision Trees: Medical Diagnosis
text:
8.1 Expected Information Gain
This chapter has two parts: a hand-built Expected Information Gain (EIG) calculation showing how a decision tree chooses which feature to split on, and a study of how hyperparameters (max depth, minimum samples) affect a tree diagnosing breast cancer from the Wisconsin Diagnostic dataset.
Code
fig_dt0 = go.Figure(data=[go.Table(
    header=dict(values=["Split Feature", "EIG (log₂)", "Interpretation"],
                fill_color="#4e79a7", font=dict(color="white", size=13), align="center"),
    cells=dict(values=[dt_eig_df["Split Feature"], dt_eig_df["EIG (log₂)"], dt_eig_df["Entropy Reduction"]],
               fill_color=[["#f0f4f8", "white", "#d4edda"]], align="center", font=dict(size=13))
)])
fig_dt0.update_layout(title="Expected Information Gain — Spam Email Decision Tree",
                      height=230, template="plotly_white")


                        
                                            


Code
fig_dt0.show()
Expected Information Gain (EIG) measures how much a proposed split reduces entropy — the uncertainty in the class labels. H(S) ≈ 0.991 for the root node (9 samples, 4 spam / 5 not-spam). EIG = H(S) − weighted average entropy after the split. The three candidate split features yield very different EIG values: Sender in Contact achieves EIG ≈ 0.590, far outperforming the other two. A split on this feature almost perfectly separates spam from non-spam, while Has Attachment (EIG = 0.007) is nearly useless as a split criterion. Decision trees greedily select the feature with the highest EIG at each node, which is why Sender in Contact would be chosen as the root split.

title: 8  Decision Trees: Medical Diagnosis

8.2 Hyperparameter Effects
Four decision trees are trained on the breast cancer data with different complexity-control settings, then compared.

Accuracy ComparisonConfusion Matrices

Each bar is the test-set accuracy (80/20 split, random_state=887) for one of the four tree configurations on the Wisconsin Breast Cancer Diagnostic dataset (569 patients, 30 cell nucleus features, binary target: Malignant vs Benign). Max Depth = 1 severely restricts the tree to a single split — it can only ask one yes/no question, leading to significant underfitting. The other three configurations allow the tree to grow deeper and capture more complex feature interactions. The Min Samples Split = 10 model achieves the highest accuracy, showing that requiring at least 10 samples before splitting provides a good bias-variance balance for this dataset.

Four confusion matrices are shown side-by-side — one per tree configuration. Each matrix’s rows are true diagnoses (B=Benign, M=Malignant) and columns are predicted diagnoses. Diagonal values are correct classifications; off-diagonal values are errors. In medical diagnosis, false negatives (malignant tumors predicted as benign) are especially costly. The Max Depth = 1 tree produces the most false negatives because it can only ask one question about the data. The more flexible trees make fewer errors overall, with Min Samples Split = 10 achieving the best combination of true positives and true negatives.









Key Findings — Decision Trees



Dataset: UCI Breast Cancer Wisconsin Diagnostic (id=17) — 569 patients, 30 features Task: Binary classification — Malignant (M) vs Benign (B) · Entropy criterion, random_state=887, 80/20 split

May overfit




EIG: for the spam example, “Sender in Contact” (EIG = 0.590) is the best root split — it reduces entropy the most, nearly perfectly separating spam from non-spam with a single question.
Why Max Depth = 1 is worst: restricting to one split means the tree can only partition the 30-feature space with a single boundary — fundamentally insufficient for distinguishing the overlapping morphological profiles of benign and malignant tumors.
Why Min Samples Split = 10 wins: requiring at least 10 samples before any split prevents the tree from creating very small, noisy leaf nodes that memorize training data. This reduces overfitting without sacrificing the flexibility needed to capture real patterns.
